24	# ── Status / type value sets (TEXT + CHECK; not native PG enums for migration ergonomics) ──
25	CONNECTION_TYPES = ("snowflake", "adls_gen2", "s3", "unity_catalog", "adf", "airflow")
26	RUN_STATUSES = ("queued", "running", "succeeded", "failed", "cancelled")
27	# Result statuses. The four severity tiers (ADR 0005) are health-score-bearing —
28	# the score aggregate sums their weights over N. The two operational statuses
29	# (#122) are orthogonal: 'skip' = not evaluated, 'error' = evaluation threw
30	# (distinct from 'fail', a successful evaluation that breached). Operational
31	# statuses carry NO penalty weight and MUST be excluded from the health-score N
32	# (i.e. aggregate WHERE status IN the four tiers only).
33	_RESULT_SEVERITY_TIERS = ("pass", "warn", "fail", "critical")
34	_RESULT_OPERATIONAL_STATUSES = ("skip", "error")
35	RESULT_STATUSES = _RESULT_SEVERITY_TIERS + _RESULT_OPERATIONAL_STATUSES
36	# Monitor-kind discriminator (ADR 0012; `comparison` reserved by ADR 0014). v1
37	# only ever writes 'expectation'; the rest are constraint-valid but unused.
38	CHECK_KINDS = ("expectation", "freshness", "volume", "schema_drift", "anomaly", "comparison")

132	class ConnectionVersion(Base):
133	    """An immutable snapshot of a connection's editable, **non-secret** state,
134	    written on create and after every successful name/config update — the source
135	    for the connection "version history" view. Mirrors `check_versions`: per-entity
136	    config history, not the cross-entity audit log (deferred — see #310).
137	
138	    Deliberately omits the credential: the secret lives only in the SecretStore
139	    (referenced by `secret_ref`, which is the constant `conn-<id>` pointer, not the
140	    value), so it is never copied here. A credential rotation (`reauth`, or an
141	    update that only changes the secret) therefore records **no** version — that is
142	    an audit-log concern, not config history.
143	
144	    `version_no` is a per-connection sequence starting at 1 (unique with
145	    `connection_id`). A version is cascade-deleted with its connection (history is
146	    not retained past deletion — accepted), but survives its author (`changed_by`
147	    is `SET NULL`).
148	    """
149	
150	    __tablename__ = "connection_versions"
151	    __table_args__ = (
152	        UniqueConstraint("connection_id", "version_no", name="uq_connection_versions_conn_version"),
153	        Index("ix_connection_versions_connection_id", "connection_id"),
154	    )
155	
156	    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = _uuid_pk()
157	    connection_id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
158	        UUID(as_uuid=True), ForeignKey("connections.id", ondelete="CASCADE"), nullable=False
159	    )
160	    version_no: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(Integer, nullable=False)
161	    # Snapshot of the editable fields. type/env are immutable on a connection but
162	    # snapshotted for a self-contained record; `config` is the non-secret
163	    # datasource config as stored. No credential / secret_ref (see class docstring).
164	    name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(128), nullable=False)
165	    type: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(32), nullable=False)
166	    env: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(16), nullable=False)
167	    config: Mapped[dict[str, Any]] = mapped_column(JSONB, nullable=False)
168	    # Who authored this version. NULL for a system/unknown actor or once the user
169	    # is removed — the snapshot must outlive its author (SET NULL, not CASCADE).
170	    changed_by: Mapped[uuid.UUID | None] = mapped_column(
171	        UUID(as_uuid=True), ForeignKey("users.id", ondelete="SET NULL")
172	    )
173	    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = _created_at()
174	
175	    author: Mapped["User | None"] = relationship()
176	
177	    @property
178	    def changed_by_name(self) -> str | None:
179	        """The author's display name (or email) for the history view, or None for
180	        a system actor / removed user. Reads the eager-loaded `author` — callers
181	        that serialize this must `selectinload(ConnectionVersion.author)`."""
182	        return (self.author.display_name or self.author.email) if self.author else None

455	    pipeline_or_dag_id: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(256), nullable=False)
456	    env: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(16), nullable=False)

458	        UUID(as_uuid=True), ForeignKey("suites.id", ondelete="CASCADE"), nullable=False
459	    )
460	    enabled: Mapped[bool] = mapped_column(Boolean, nullable=False, server_default=text("true"))
461	    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = _created_at()
462	    updated_at: Mapped[datetime] = _updated_at()
463	
464	
465	class Schedule(Base):
466	    """A cron schedule that fires a suite run automatically (A7).
467	
468	    The beat dispatcher (`worker.tasks.dispatch_due_schedules`) ticks every
469	    minute and queries `enabled AND next_run_at <= now()` — an indexed scan, so
470	    the cron is parsed only when a schedule actually fires, never per tick.
471	
472	    `next_run_at` is precomputed (`services.cron.next_fire`) on create and after
473	    each fire / cron change. **No-backfill semantics**: a fire advances it to the
474	    next *future* occurrence, so a downtime gap fires at most once on recovery
475	    rather than backfilling every missed slot (correct for monitoring). `cron` is
476	    a standard 5-field expression evaluated in `timezone` (IANA, DST-aware,
477	    default UTC). Cascade-deleted with the suite.
478	    """
479	
480	    __tablename__ = "schedules"
481	    __table_args__ = (
482	        Index("ix_schedules_suite_id", "suite_id"),
483	        # The dispatcher's hot path: due + enabled schedules, oldest-due first.
484	        Index("ix_schedules_enabled_next_run_at", "enabled", "next_run_at"),
485	    )
486	
487	    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = _uuid_pk()
488	    suite_id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
489	        UUID(as_uuid=True), ForeignKey("suites.id", ondelete="CASCADE"), nullable=False
490	    )
491	    cron: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(128), nullable=False)
492	    # IANA tz name the cron is evaluated in (e.g. 'America/New_York'); 'UTC' default.
493	    timezone: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(64), nullable=False, server_default=text("'UTC'"))
494	    enabled: Mapped[bool] = mapped_column(Boolean, nullable=False, server_default=text("true"))
495	    # Precomputed next fire (UTC). The dispatcher scans on this; never parses cron.
496	    next_run_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(DateTime(timezone=True), nullable=False)
497	    # Last time the dispatcher fired this schedule (NULL until first fire).
498	    last_run_at: Mapped[datetime | None] = mapped_column(DateTime(timezone=True))
499	    created_by: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
500	        UUID(as_uuid=True), ForeignKey("users.id"), nullable=False
501	    )
502	    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = _created_at()
503	    updated_at: Mapped[datetime] = _updated_at()
504	
505	
506	class SuiteNotification(Base):
507	    """Per-suite alert delivery config (one row per suite).
508	
509	    Decides *whether* a suite's run outcomes are delivered (``enabled``), at what
510	    threshold (``alert_on``), and *where* — an optional per-suite Teams webhook
511	    referenced via ``webhook_secret_ref`` (the URL is token-bearing, so it lives
512	    in the SecretStore, never the DB), falling back to the workspace webhook when
513	    NULL. Suites with no row use the default policy (alert on warn+). The Teams
514	    publisher reads this when delivering (``alerting.teams``). Cascade-deleted
515	    with the suite.
516	    """
517	
518	    __tablename__ = "suite_notifications"
519	    __table_args__ = (
520	        _in_check("alert_on", ALERT_ON_POLICIES, "alert_on_valid"),
521	        UniqueConstraint("suite_id", name="uq_suite_notifications_suite_id"),
522	    )
523	
524	    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = _uuid_pk()
525	    suite_id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
526	        UUID(as_uuid=True), ForeignKey("suites.id", ondelete="CASCADE"), nullable=False
527	    )
528	    enabled: Mapped[bool] = mapped_column(Boolean, nullable=False, server_default=text("true"))
529	    # Delivery threshold (ADR 0005 tiers): 'fail' = fail/critical only, 'warn' =
530	    # warn+, 'always' = every terminal run. Default 'warn' matches the no-config
531	    # fallback (`notification_service.DEFAULT_ALERT_ON`) so saving a config
532	    # doesn't silently change the threshold. See `alerting.routing.route_for`.
533	    alert_on: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(16), nullable=False, server_default=text("'warn'"))
534	    # SecretStore key for the per-suite Teams webhook URL (NULL → workspace webhook).
535	    # The URL is a secret; only the ref is stored here.
536	    webhook_secret_ref: Mapped[str | None] = mapped_column(String(256))
537	    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = _created_at()
538	    updated_at: Mapped[datetime] = _updated_at()
539


### Reading notes

- **Conventions (elided from the diagram for noise):** every table has a `gen_random_uuid()` UUID PK and `created_at`; mutable entities also carry `updated_at`. Status/type columns are `TEXT` + `CHECK` constraints, **not** native PG enums (migration ergonomics).
- **Cascade posture (ADR [0020](adr/0020-history-and-audit-strategy.md)):** deleting a suite cascades its checks, runs, results, shares, trigger bindings, schedules, and notification config; deleting a connection cascades its version history. History is not retained past entity deletion — accepted. Version snapshots survive their *author* (`changed_by` is `SET NULL`), not their entity.
- **`pipeline_runs` ≠ `runs` — no FK between them.** Orchestrator pipeline executions correlate to the DQ suite runs they trigger only via the string marker `runs.triggered_by = '<provider>:<pipeline_or_dag_id>:<provider_run_id>'` (dotted lines above); `trigger_bindings` matches pipeline runs by `(provider, pipeline_or_dag_id, env)`, also without an FK. A partial unique index on `runs (suite_id, triggered_by)` dedupes orchestration-triggered runs.
- **Singleton constraints:** at most one orchestrator connection per `(type, env)` (partial unique index over `adf`/`airflow` only — datasources may repeat); one `suite_notifications` row per suite; one live `shares` row per `(suite, user)`.
- **Secrets are never in these tables.** `connections.secret_ref` / `suite_notifications.webhook_secret_ref` hold SecretStore *keys*; version snapshots deliberately omit credentials, so a credential rotation records no version.

## Key invariants

content.text:
Added a **Data model (ER diagram)** section to [architecture.md](docs/architecture.md), generated from the actual SQLAlchemy models in [models.py](backend/app/db/models.py) and validated with the Mermaid renderer (it parses clean as an `erDiagram`, so it will render on GitHub).

What's in it:

- **All 13 tables** with their meaningful columns, PK/FK/UK markers, and inline comments for the constrained value sets (connection types, run/result statuses, check kinds, permissions, alert policies). Boilerplate `id`/`created_at`/`updated_at` conventions are stated once in prose instead of repeated per table, to keep the diagram readable.
- **Real FK relationships** with their delete behavior labeled — the suite-rooted CASCADE tree (checks, runs, results, shares, trigger_bindings, schedules, suite_notifications), connection→version history, and the `SET NULL` author links on the two version-snapshot tables.
- **The two soft (no-FK) correlations drawn as dotted lines** — `pipeline_runs ⇢ runs` via the `triggered_by` string marker and `trigger_bindings ⇢ pipeline_runs` via the `(provider, pipeline_or_dag_id, env)` match — since the `pipeline_runs ≠ runs` distinction is the thing this codebase most wants readers not to get wrong.
- A short **Reading notes** subsection covering the cascade posture (ADR 0020), the orchestrator-singleton partial unique index, and the "secrets are refs, never values" rule.

I also updated the doc's header note to say "diagrams" (plural) so the sync-in-same-PR rule explicitly covers the ER diagram, and the new section carries its own pointer to `models.py` as the source of truth.

The change is uncommitted on `main` — say the word if you want me to branch, commit (`docs: add data-model ER diagram to architecture.md`), and open a PR per the usual flow.
Anything similar we're missing from the standard or best practice point of view?
Let me take stock of what documentation artifacts the repo already has before saying what's missing.

# Observability & troubleshooting

## Logs & telemetry

- **Structured JSON logs** (structlog) everywhere, with a `request_id` correlated across
  FastAPI → Celery → Great Expectations. **PII is redacted at the logger level**, so
  failed-check sample rows can't leak into logs.
- **Azure Application Insights** captures logs + exceptions in deployed environments
  (gated on the connection string; off locally).
- **Request + task spans** (OpenTelemetry, Azure-exported) in deployed environments:
  every API request and Celery task run is a trace in App Insights, linked
  request → task, with a `dataq.request_id` attribute joining spans to the log lines.
  Health probes and the secret-bearing webhook URLs are excluded by design.

## Where to look

- **Dashboard** — workspace health score, pass-rate, run counts, and trends.
- **Results** — every run, drill into per-check pass/fail with observed-vs-expected and
  (PII-redacted) sample failing rows; a **Pipelines** tab correlates orchestration runs.
- **Run progress** — live check-by-check status for an in-flight run.

## Common failures

| Symptom | Likely cause / fix |
|---|---|
| Run stuck `queued` | Broker (Redis) unreachable at dispatch. A beat **reaper** fails runs left non-terminal past a threshold; re-run once the worker/broker is healthy. |
| Connection **Test** fails | Bad/expired credential or network egress — re-authenticate the connection; check the secret store. |
| Pipeline didn't trigger a suite | Only **successful** runs trigger; confirm an **enabled trigger binding** matches `(provider, pipeline/DAG, env)` exactly (the DAG/pipeline id is case-sensitive). |
| Checks all `skip` on a flat-file suite | The batch hasn't landed yet (no matching file) — expected; the run succeeds with skips. |
| Orchestration events not arriving | Webhook secret/HMAC mismatch — the 10-min poll is the fallback; verify the connection's secret, and copy the URL from **Settings → Webhooks** instead of assembling it. |

Good question — I inventoried `docs/`, `deploy/`, and `.github/` first. The repo is already well past standard practice on most fronts (ADRs with an index, runbook/FAQ, observability guide, deployment guide, compliance posture, glossary, feature matrix, retro, SECURITY.md, Swagger for the API surface). The real gaps are narrow, and they're all in the same family as the ER diagram: **the docs describe structure well but never show behavior over time.** Ranked by value:

1. **Sequence diagrams for the two load-bearing runtime flows.** Nothing in the repo shows ordering/time — the flowchart shows who talks to whom, the ER diagram shows what's stored, but the choreography lives only in prose scattered across ADRs. The two that earn their keep:
   - **Suite run lifecycle:** trigger (manual / schedule / webhook) → API → Celery dispatch → GX execution → severity derivation (ADR 0016) → results → `ResultPublisher` alert routing. This crosses every component and is the flow a new contributor has to reconstruct by reading five services.
   - **Orchestration event flow:** ADF/Airflow webhook → auth check (secret / HMAC) → `pipeline_runs` upsert → trigger-binding match → run dispatch, with the 10-min poll fallback and the dedup index as annotations. It's the flow with the most "critical pointers" warnings in CLAUDE.md, which is a signal it's under-diagrammed.

2. **A state diagram for run and result statuses.** Tiny, but the semantics are subtle and load-bearing: `skip`/`error` are orthogonal to the four severity tiers and must be excluded from the health-score N — that rule currently lives in a code comment in [models.py:27-35](backend/app/db/models.py#L27-L35). A ~15-line `stateDiagram` in architecture.md (or concepts.md) makes it un-missable.

3. **A drift guard for the ER diagram.** Best practice for hand-drawn ERDs is either generate-from-metadata or test-against-metadata. The "update in the same PR" note relies on discipline; a small pytest that asserts every `Base.metadata` table name appears in `architecture.md` (and flags tables in the doc that no longer exist) would catch drift in CI for ~20 lines. Column-level checking is overkill; table-level is the right altitude.

4. **A CHANGELOG / release notes.** `v1.0.0` is tagged but there's no `CHANGELOG.md` and (as far as the repo shows) no GitHub Release body. With conventional commits enforced, generating one is nearly free, and it matters for the BYOL/marketplace ambition (ADR 0013) — customers deploying your image will ask "what changed in v1.1?"

5. **A trust-boundary / auth-flow diagram** (threat-model lite). The auth story is complete but scattered across ADRs 0006, 0007, 0008, and 0028: browser OIDC, MCP bearer, webhook secret-in-URL vs HMAC, internal-vs-public ingress. One diagram showing the trust boundaries would consolidate it — and it's the artifact a marketplace security review or customer security questionnaire asks for first.

Things I'd explicitly *not* add: full C4 formality (your flowchart already is a container diagram; more layers is ceremony), a hand-written API reference (Swagger owns that), and component-level class diagrams of the service layer (they rot fast and the code is readable).

If you want, I can do #1 and #2 now — they're pure additions to architecture.md in the same style as the ER diagram, validated the same way. #3 is a small test PR, #4 is a one-off backfill plus a release-notes habit, and #5 is probably worth doing as part of the pre-marketplace hardening rather than today.
do all five. The point four i'm thinking more of for major [maybe including some minor] releases not for every PR, what say you?

All five it is. On the point-4 cadence question, my answer up front: **agree — release-time, not per-PR.** Per-PR changelog lines are exactly the kind of churn Keep-a-Changelog warns about (merge conflicts, entries nobody curates), and your squash-merge + conventional-commits discipline means `git log v1.0.0..v1.1.0 --oneline` regenerates the raw material on demand. So the policy I'll encode: **one curated entry per minor/major release at tag time; patch releases only get an entry when user-visible or security-relevant.** I'll write that into the CHANGELOG header and the release checklist in the runbook.

Now let me ground the sequence/state/trust diagrams in the actual code so they're accurate, not aspirational.

12	``run_target.resolve_target``): a targetless suite drives the run to ``failed``
13	with a clear log rather than running against an unknown table.

45	# interval so a run can't slip through the gap between consecutive polls.
46	_POLL_LOOKBACK = timedelta(minutes=15)

48	# runs missed while the system was down (worker/beat restart, webhook + poll both

58	    """Drive ``run`` to terminal ``failed`` (never left ``queued``/``running``)."""
59	    run.status = "failed"

68	    """Load the run's graph, resolve its target, build the runner, execute.
69

71	    ``(table, schema, catalog)`` via ``run_target.resolve_target``; dispatch by

73	    Catalog / flat-file suite its correct `CheckRunner` (#146). A flat-file *batch*

128	        return _terminal_failed(session, run, event="run_suite_materialize_failed", run_id=run_id)

295	def _advance_schedule(schedule: Schedule, *, now: datetime) -> bool:
296	    """Roll ``schedule`` forward to its next future fire and stamp ``last_run_at``.
297	
298	    **No-backfill semantics**: ``cron.next_fire`` returns the next occurrence
299	    strictly after ``now``, so a gap (worker/beat down across several slots) is
300	    collapsed to a single fire rather than backfilled. Returns True if advanced;
301	    False (and disables the schedule) if the stored cron/tz is somehow invalid —
302	    validated on write, so this only guards against direct DB tampering and stops
303	    an un-advanceable row from hot-looping the dispatcher every tick.
304	    """
305	    schedule.last_run_at = now
306	    try:
307	        schedule.next_run_at = cron.next_fire(schedule.cron, schedule.timezone, after=now)
308	    except DataQError:
309	        schedule.enabled = False
310	        log.error(
311	            "schedule_disabled_invalid_cron",
312	            schedule_id=str(schedule.id),
313	            cron=schedule.cron,
314	            timezone=schedule.timezone,
315	        )
316	        return False
317	    return True

320	def _fire_schedule(session: Session, schedule: Schedule, *, now: datetime) -> str:
321	    """Fire one due schedule: advance it, then queue + dispatch a suite run.
322	
323	    Advancing ``next_run_at`` happens **before** the run is created and is
324	    committed in every branch, so the schedule leaves the due window for this
325	    tick whatever the run's fate — a misconfigured suite never hot-loops. The run
326	    is created with the canonical ``schedule:<id>`` ``triggered_by`` marker and
327	    handed to the worker exactly like the manual / pipeline-trigger paths; a
328	    targetless suite is skipped (not queued-then-failed), and a broker outage
329	    marks the run ``failed`` rather than leaving it stuck ``queued`` (#227).
330	    """
331	    if not _advance_schedule(schedule, now=now):
332	        session.commit()
333	        return "disabled"
334	
335	    suite = session.get(Suite, schedule.suite_id)
336	    assert suite is not None  # schedule cascade-deletes with its suite
337	    connection = session.get(Connection, suite.connection_id)
338	    assert connection is not None  # suite.connection_id FK is RESTRICT
339	    try:
340	        run_target.resolve_target(connection.type, suite.target)
341	    except DataQError:
342	        session.commit()  # persist the advance; skip the doomed run
343	        log.warning(
344	            "schedule_skipped_invalid_target",
345	            schedule_id=str(schedule.id),
346	            suite_id=str(suite.id),
347	        )
348	        return "skipped_target"
349	
350	    run = Run(suite_id=suite.id, status="queued", triggered_by=f"schedule:{schedule.id}")
351	    session.add(run)
352	    session.commit()
353	    session.refresh(run)
354	    # Shared dispatch+broker-failure block (#227): on failure the run is marked
355	    # terminal-`failed` and logged (with schedule_id kept on the event); the
356	    # advance is already committed, so the schedule has left the due window.
357	    if not run_dispatch.dispatch_or_fail(session, run, schedule_id=str(schedule.id)):
358	        return "dispatch_failed"
359	    log.info("schedule_fired", schedule_id=str(schedule.id), run_id=str(run.id))
360	    return "dispatched"
361	
362	
363	def _dispatch_due_schedules(session: Session, *, now: datetime | None = None) -> dict[str, int]:
364	    """Fire every enabled schedule whose ``next_run_at`` has passed (A7).
365	
366	    Pulls due schedules one at a time with ``FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`` so two
367	    overlapping dispatcher ticks can't double-fire the same schedule: the second
368	    skips a row the first holds, and once fired the row's ``next_run_at`` is past
369	    ``now`` so it drops out of the due set. ``now`` is fixed at entry, so the loop
370	    is finite (each iteration advances one row out of the window).
371	    """
372	    now = now or datetime.now(UTC)
373	    summary = {"due": 0, "dispatched": 0, "skipped_target": 0, "dispatch_failed": 0, "disabled": 0}
374	    while True:
375	        schedule = session.scalars(
376	            select(Schedule)
377	            .where(Schedule.enabled.is_(True), Schedule.next_run_at <= now)
378	            .order_by(Schedule.next_run_at)
379	            .with_for_update(skip_locked=True)
380	            .limit(1)
381	        ).first()
382	        if schedule is None:
383	            break
384	        summary["due"] += 1
385	        outcome = _fire_schedule(session, schedule, now=now)
386	        summary[outcome] = summary.get(outcome, 0) + 1
387	    log.info("schedules_dispatch_completed", **summary)
388	    return summary
389	
390	
391	@celery_app.task(name="dispatch_due_schedules")  # type: ignore[untyped-decorator]  # celery task decorator is unannotated
392	def dispatch_due_schedules() -> dict[str, int]:
393	    """Celery-beat entry point — fire due suite-run schedules (A7), every minute."""
394	    session = get_session()
395	    try:
396	        return _dispatch_due_schedules(session)
397	    finally:
398	        session.close()

405	def purge_sample_failures() -> int:
406	    """Celery-beat entry point — daily PII-retention sweep.

408	    Scrubs `sample_failures` from results older than the configured
409	    ``sample_failures_retention_days`` window (keeping the row + `metric_value` so
410	    trends survive — ADR 0012). Returns the number of rows scrubbed.
411	    """
412	    session = get_session()
413	    try:
414	        retention_days = get_settings().sample_failures_retention_days
415	        return run_service.purge_expired_sample_failures(session, retention_days=retention_days)
416	    finally:
417	        session.close()

420	# ──────────────────────── stuck-run reaper (#309) ──────────────────────────
421	
422	
423	@celery_app.task(name="reap_stuck_runs")  # type: ignore[untyped-decorator]  # celery task decorator is unannotated
424	def reap_stuck_runs() -> int:
425	    """Celery-beat entry point — fail runs orphaned in a non-terminal state (#309).
426	
427	    A run committed ``queued`` before its task was published — or left ``running``
428	    by a worker that died mid-execution — would otherwise linger forever (gap
429	    recovery only covers ``pipeline_runs``). The reaper drives such runs, stuck
430	    past ``stuck_run_threshold_minutes``, to terminal ``failed`` so they surface in
431	    the runs table / dashboard and the user can re-run. No alert is published — see
432	    ``run_service.reap_stuck_runs`` for why (a reaped run is an infra/liveness
433	    event, and alerting a slow-but-alive run would be an irreversible false alarm).
434	    Returns the count reaped.
435	    """
436	    session = get_session()
437	    try:
438	        threshold = get_settings().stuck_run_threshold_minutes
439	        return len(run_service.reap_stuck_runs(session, threshold_minutes=threshold))
440	    finally:

2	
3	The single home for the ADR-0005 tier derivation + the ADR-0012 `metric_value`

7	
8	Model (ADR 0016, approach A):
9	- `metric_value` = the GX **unexpected percent** (0-100): how badly the rule was
10	  violated. 0 = clean, higher = worse. It is the SQL-aggregatable badness scalar
11	  (ADR 0012) and the quantity the thresholds band.
12	- When a check has thresholds, they are the user's severity policy and they

15	  `warn` threshold is 1%). The stored `metric_value` keeps this transparent.
16	- No thresholds set, or no metric to band (aggregate checks like row_count that
17	  produce no unexpected fraction) → binary `pass`/`fail` from GX success
18	  (ADR 0005 binary fallback).

41	    ``Decimal(str(...))`` so a float like ``0.5`` lands as exact ``0.5`` in the
42	    NUMERIC column rather than its binary expansion.
43	    """
44	    raw: Any
45	    if outcome.metric_value is not None:
46	        raw = outcome.metric_value
47	    else:
48	        sample: dict[str, Any] | None = outcome.sample_failures
49	        if not sample or _UNEXPECTED_PERCENT_KEY not in sample:
50	            return None
51	        raw = sample[_UNEXPECTED_PERCENT_KEY]

63	    return metric if metric.is_finite() else None

input.new_string:
- **Secrets are never in these tables.** `connections.secret_ref` / `suite_notifications.webhook_secret_ref` hold SecretStore *keys*; version snapshots deliberately omit credentials, so a credential rotation records no version. ## Runtime flows The diagrams above show *structure* (who talks to whom, what is stored); these two show *ordering* for the flows that cross the most components. ### Suite run lifecycle Every run — manual (`POST /suites/{id}/run`), scheduled (the 60s beat dispatcher), or orchestration-triggered — converges on the same path once the `Run` row exists: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant Trig as Trigger
(manual API · schedule beat · pipeline event) participant API as FastAPI / beat dispatcher participant PG as PostgreSQL participant BR as Redis broker participant W as Celery worker (run_suite) participant KV as SecretStore (Key Vault) participant DS as Datasource participant Pub as ResultPublisher (Teams · Slack · email) Trig->>API: trigger suite run API->>PG: INSERT runs (status=queued, triggered_by marker) API->>BR: send_task run_suite(run_id) Note over API,BR: broker down → run marked terminal failed,
never left stuck queued (#227) API->>PG: store celery_task_id (enables cancel/revoke) BR->>W: deliver task W->>PG: load run — already cancelled? stop (cooperative cancel) W->>PG: load suite + connection + checks, resolve target (#215) W->>KV: get connection credential (secret_ref) W->>W: build CheckRunner by connection.type (registry, ADR 0011) Note over W,KV: any setup failure → run terminal failed W->>DS: materialize flat-file batch path Note over W,DS: batch absent → every check skip,
run still succeeds (#122) W->>PG: UPDATE runs SET status=running W->>DS: execute by check.kind (ADR 0012) —
expectation → GX validate · freshness/volume → monitor SQL DS-->>W: one CheckOutcome per check W->>W: severity.resolve_status — band unexpected-% against
warn/fail/critical thresholds (ADR 0005/0016) W->>PG: INSERT results (status, metric_value, redacted sample_failures) W->>PG: UPDATE runs SET status=succeeded
(failed = the adapter raised) W->>Pub: publish_run_outcome — snooze suppression → alert_on routing → dedup → publish Note over W,Pub: best-effort — a publish failure never
affects the persisted run ``` Key sources: [`worker/tasks.py`](../backend/app/worker/tasks.py) (`run_suite`), [`services/run_service.py`](../backend/app/services/run_service.py) (`execute_run`), [`services/run_dispatch.py`](../backend/app/services/run_dispatch.py), [`services/severity.py`](../backend/app/services/severity.py), [`alerting/dispatch.py`](../backend/app/alerting/dispatch.py). ### Orchestration event flow How an ADF / Airflow pipeline outcome becomes (at most) a triggered suite run. Everything goes through the `OrchestrationProvider` seam (ADR [0004](adr/0004-orchestration-abstraction.md)) — no provider branching: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant ORC as ADF / Airflow participant FE as Frontend nginx (public ingress) participant API as FastAPI (internal) participant KV as SecretStore (Key Vault) participant PG as PostgreSQL participant BR as Redis broker participant W as Celery worker Note over ORC: pipeline / DAG run reaches a terminal state ORC->>FE: POST /api/v1/orchestration/events/{provider}
(ADF — Azure Monitor alert · Airflow — on_*_callback snippet) FE->>API: same-origin proxy API->>KV: load receiver secret API->>API: authenticate — ADF constant-time token query param (ADR 0006)
· Airflow HMAC-SHA256 over raw body (ADR 0007) API->>API: OrchestrationProvider.parse → RunUpdate or AlertPing alt AlertPing — run-anonymous Azure Monitor alert (#492) API->>BR: enqueue targeted poll-now (provider + resource) API-->>ORC: 202 reconciling else RunUpdate API->>PG: upsert pipeline_runs ON (provider, provider_run_id) alt status = succeeded API->>PG: match enabled trigger_bindings (provider, pipeline_or_dag_id, env) API->>PG: INSERT runs, triggered_by = provider:pipeline:run_id
ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (dedup index, #308) API->>BR: dispatch run_suite per triggered run else status = failed API->>API: alert the user only — failures never trigger suites end API-->>ORC: 200 recorded end Note over W,PG: polling fallback (#171) — a 10-min beat sweeps every orchestrator connection
via the provider REST API (list_recent_runs, 15-min lookback) into the SAME
upsert + trigger path; a 30-min gap-recovery sweep (1-hour window) covers downtime ``` Key sources: [`api/v1/orchestration.py`](../backend/app/api/v1/orchestration.py), [`services/orchestration_service.py`](../backend/app/services/orchestration_service.py), [`worker/tasks.py`](../backend/app/worker/tasks.py) (`poll_orchestration_runs`, `recover_orchestration_gaps`). ## Status semantics ### Run lifecycle `runs.status` describes **execution, not data quality** — a run whose checks all failed is still `succeeded`. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> queued : run created (manual · schedule · trigger binding) queued --> running : worker picks up the task queued --> cancelled : user cancel — task revoked + cooperative start-check queued --> failed : dispatch / setup / target failure — or reaper past threshold (#309) running --> succeeded : execution completed (even when checks FAILED — run status is execution, not data quality) running --> failed : adapter raised — or worker died and the reaper swept it (#309) running --> cancelled : user cancel (DB status set; worker honours cooperatively) succeeded --> [*] failed --> [*] cancelled --> [*] ``` ### Result status derivation `results.status` has two orthogonal families: the four **severity tiers** (ADR [0005](adr/0005-severity-tier-weights.md)) and the two **operational statuses** (#122). Only the tiers carry health-score weight (0.5 / 1.0 / 2.0 for warn / fail / critical); `skip`/`error` **must be excluded from the health-score denominator**. ```mermaid flowchart TD O["CheckOutcome (from the runner)"] --> E{"runner could evaluate it?"} E -- "no — evaluation raised" --> ERR["error — operational (#122)
no tier · no metric · error message in observed_value"] E -- yes --> S{"flat-file batch landed?
(decided before execution)"} S -- no --> SKIP["skip — operational (#122)
not evaluated at all"] S -- yes --> M{"thresholds set AND a
bandable metric_value exists?"} M -- "no — binary fallback (ADR 0005)" --> BIN{"GX success?"} BIN -- yes --> PASS[pass] BIN -- no --> FAIL[fail] M -- yes --> BAND["band unexpected-% (0-100, higher = worse)
against warn / fail / critical thresholds (ADR 0016)"] BAND --> TIER["pass / warn / fail / critical
thresholds are policy — they OVERRIDE GX success"] classDef op fill:#F1EFE8,stroke:#5F5E5A,color:#444441 classDef tier fill:#E6F1FB,stroke:#185FA5,color:#0C449C class ERR,SKIP op class PASS,FAIL,TIER tier ``` The single decision lives in [`services/severity.py`](../backend/app/services/severity.py) (`resolve_status`), shared by run persistence and the check-editor dry-run so a preview can never disagree with the run it previews. ## Trust boundaries & authentication The one-diagram consolidation of ADRs [0006](adr/0006-adf-webhook-authentication.md) / [0007](adr/0007-airflow-callback-model.md) / [0008](adr/0008-mcp-server.md) / [0028](adr/0028-cloud-neutral-image-runtime-config-generic-oidc.md) — what crosses each boundary and what credential it carries: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph internet["🌍 Untrusted — public internet"] B["Browser (React SPA)"] AI["AI clients (MCP)"] WH["ADF / Airflow webhooks"] end IDP["🔑 OIDC authority
(Azure AD behind the generic DATAQ_AUTH_* contract)"] subgraph aca["🟦 Trust boundary — Container Apps env"] FE["Frontend nginx — SOLE public ingress (TLS)
serves the SPA + runtime auth config (non-secret)"] API["FastAPI — internal ingress only
validates EVERY request itself (no EasyAuth)"] WK["Celery worker — no ingress"] end subgraph backing["🔒 Credentialed backing services"] KV["Key Vault (all connection + webhook secrets)"] PG[("PostgreSQL — dataq db,
least-priv dataq_app role")] RD[("Redis — password auth")] APPI["App Insights (PII-redacted logs + traces)"] end subgraph egress["🌐 Outbound — credentials fetched from Key Vault per use"] DS["Datasources — Snowflake · ADLS · S3 · Unity Catalog"] AL["Teams / Slack webhooks · SMTP"] OAPI["ADF / Airflow REST APIs (polling)"] end B -- "OIDC auth-code + PKCE" --> IDP AI -- "same bearer token" --> IDP B -- "HTTPS · bearer JWT on /api" --> FE AI -- "bearer JWT on /mcp" --> FE WH -- "ADF: shared-secret token in URL (ADR 0006)
Airflow: HMAC-SHA256 body signature (ADR 0007)" --> FE FE -- "same-origin proxy /api · /mcp · /healthz (HTTP/1.1)" --> API API -- "JWT validated (fastapi-azure-auth / MCP JWTVerifier)
+ per-suite authz + sample redaction" --> API API -- "enqueue tasks" --> RD RD -- "deliver tasks" --> WK API -- "UAMI — no stored credential" --> KV WK -- "UAMI — no stored credential" --> KV API --> PG WK --> PG API --> APPI WK --> APPI WK -- "GX checks / monitor SQL" --> DS WK -- "run-outcome alerts" --> AL WK -- "10-min poll fallback" --> OAPI classDef hub fill:#E6F1FB,stroke:#185FA5,color:#0C449C classDef ext fill:#F1EFE8,stroke:#5F5E5A,color:#444441 classDef sec fill:#FAEEDA,stroke:#854F0B,color:#633806 classDef out fill:#E1F5EE,stroke:#0F6E56,color:#085041 class FE,API,WK hub class B,AI,WH,IDP ext class KV,PG,RD,APPI sec class DS,AL,OAPI out style internet fill:#F7F6F2,stroke:#5F5E5A style aca fill:#F4F9FE,stroke:#185FA5 style backing fill:#FDF7EC,stroke:#854F0B style egress fill:#F0FBF7,stroke:#0F6E56 ``` Boundary notes: - **Defense in depth, not perimeter trust:** the API validates every request's bearer JWT itself (`fastapi-azure-auth` for REST, `JWTVerifier` for MCP — same tenant/audience/scope) even though it is only reachable through the frontend proxy. Platform-level auth (SWA EasyAuth) is explicitly disabled (#511). - **The only endpoints that bypass user JWT auth** are the two orchestration webhook receivers (each with its own secret scheme, above) and the health probe. Webhook secrets live in Key Vault and are compared constant-time; they are never logged. - **Nothing secret is baked into images or served to the browser.** The frontend's runtime `DATAQ_AUTH_*` config is non-secret OIDC metadata (ADR 0028); all real secrets resolve at use-time from Key Vault via user-assigned managed identity. - **MCP is fail-closed:** without resolvable auth config the `/mcp` mount does not come up at all (ADR 0008). ## Key invariants

input.content:
"""Drift guard: the ER diagram in docs/architecture.md tracks the real schema.

The diagram is hand-maintained (docs rule: update it in the same PR as any
model/migration change), so this pins the *table-level* contract both ways —
a model table missing from the diagram, or a diagram entity naming a table
that no longer exists, fails CI. Column-level sync stays a review concern
(checking every attribute here would just duplicate the models file).

Pure-unit: reads the markdown + `Base.metadata`; no DB.
"""

import re
from pathlib import Path

from backend.app.db import models
from backend.app.db.base import Base

_REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[3]
_ARCHITECTURE_MD = _REPO_ROOT / "docs" / "architecture.md"

# An entity definition inside the erDiagram block: `    users {`
_ENTITY_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*(\w+)\s*\{", re.MULTILINE)
_ER_BLOCK_RE = re.compile(r"```mermaid\s*\nerDiagram\n(.*?)```", re.DOTALL)


def _er_diagram_entities() -> set[str]:
    match = _ER_BLOCK_RE.search(_ARCHITECTURE_MD.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
    assert match, "docs/architecture.md has no ```mermaid erDiagram``` block"
    return set(_ENTITY_RE.findall(match.group(1)))


def test_models_module_exports_every_mapped_table() -> None:
    # Sanity for the guard itself: the metadata the tests compare against is
    # populated by importing the models module.
    assert models.Base is Base
    assert Base.metadata.tables, "Base.metadata is empty — models not registered"


def test_every_table_appears_in_the_er_diagram() -> None:
    missing = set(Base.metadata.tables) - _er_diagram_entities()
    assert not missing, (
        f"tables missing from the docs/architecture.md ER diagram: {sorted(missing)} — "
        "update the diagram in the same PR as the model/migration change"
    )


def test_er_diagram_has_no_stale_tables() -> None:
    stale = _er_diagram_entities() - set(Base.metadata.tables)
    assert not stale, (
        f"docs/architecture.md ER diagram names tables that no longer exist: {sorted(stale)} — "
        "remove them from the diagram"
    )

input.content:
# Changelog

All notable user-facing changes to DataQ are documented here. The format follows
[Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/) and the project uses
[Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/).

**Cadence:** one curated entry per **minor/major release, written at tag time** —
not per PR. Patch releases get an entry only when the change is user-visible or
security-relevant. Raw material comes from the squashed conventional commits
(`git log v<prev>..HEAD --oneline`); curate it down to what a deployer or user
would act on. The step lives in the release checklist in
[docs/runbook-faq.md](docs/runbook-faq.md).

## [Unreleased]

## [1.0.0] — 2026-07-04

First production release. Single-tenant data quality monitoring platform on
Great Expectations (GX Core), deployed to Azure Container Apps.

### Added

- **Datasources (4):** Snowflake (DEV/QA/UAT), ADLS Gen2, AWS S3, and Unity
  Catalog (Databricks) behind a common `ConnectionAdapter`/`CheckRunner` seam,
  with a connection manager UI, connection testing, re-auth, and per-connection
  version history. All credentials live in Azure Key Vault — never in the DB.
- **Checks & suites:** catalog-driven GX expectation editor + Monaco custom-SQL
  checks (read-only, single-statement guardrails — ADR 0019); freshness & volume
  monitor kinds (ADR 0012); severity tiers `warn`/`fail`/`critical` derived from
  user thresholds banding the GX unexpected-% (ADR 0005/0016); dry-run preview;
  column profiler; per-check version history; suite export/import.
- **Execution:** async Celery runs across all datasource types with live
  per-check progress, cancel, flat-file batch resolution (absent batch → skip,
  not failure), a stuck-run reaper, and cron scheduling (DST-aware, no-backfill).
- **Orchestration (monitor + trigger only, never datasources):** ADF and Apache
  Airflow behind one `OrchestrationProvider` interface — webhook receivers
  (shared-secret / HMAC-signed), a 10-minute polling fallback, downtime gap
  recovery, and trigger bindings that start suite runs on pipeline success.
- **Results & dashboard:** in-app Results page with run drill-down,
  observed-vs-expected, PII-redacted failing-row samples (column-aware policy +
  retention purge), pipeline-runs correlation, and a monitoring dashboard with
  health score, pass-rate, and trends.
- **Alerting:** MS Teams, Slack, and email publishers behind the
  `ResultPublisher` seam with severity-aware routing, dedup, per-check snooze,
  and per-suite delivery config.
- **Access model:** Azure AD SSO via a generic OIDC contract (`DATAQ_AUTH_*`,
  no MSAL — ADR 0028); suite-level sharing (`owner`/`edit`/`view`); implicit
  workspace-admin (ADR 0027) with an admin control centre.
- **AI integration:** 8 curated FastMCP tools at `/mcp`, validated with the same
  Azure AD bearer token, fail-closed without auth (ADR 0008).
- **Deployment:** in-repo Terraform for Azure Container Apps (API internal,
  frontend the sole public surface), GHCR multi-arch images, GitHub Actions
  build→migrate→deploy pipeline, App Insights logs + OpenTelemetry traces.

[Unreleased]: https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/compare/v1.0.0...HEAD
[1.0.0]: https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/releases/tag/v1.0.0

content.text:
All five are done, validated, and green locally. On your cadence question: **agreed and encoded** — the CHANGELOG header and the release checklist now both state the policy: one curated entry per minor/major release written at tag time, patch releases only when user-visible or security-relevant, raw material regenerated from `git log v<prev>..HEAD` (your squash-merged conventional commits make this cheap), mirrored into the GitHub Release body. Per-PR changelog lines are explicitly not the convention.

What landed (all in the working tree, uncommitted, alongside the earlier ER diagram):

**[architecture.md](docs/architecture.md)** — four new sections, every diagram validated through the Mermaid renderer and grounded in the actual code, not the ADR prose:
- **Runtime flows** — two sequence diagrams: the suite run lifecycle (trigger → queued run → broker → worker → SecretStore → GX/monitor execution → severity banding → results → best-effort alert publish, with the broker-down, cooperative-cancel, and batch-absent-→-skip edge cases as notes) and the orchestration event flow (webhook auth per provider, AlertPing poll-now branch, `pipeline_runs` upsert, trigger-binding match with the #308 dedup index, failure-never-triggers rule, and the 10-min poll + gap-recovery fallback). Each ends with links to the source modules.
- **Status semantics** — a state diagram for `runs.status` (including both cancel paths and the reaper) and a derivation flowchart for `results.status` making the two things easiest to get wrong un-missable: run status is execution not data quality, and `skip`/`error` are excluded from the health-score denominator.
- **Trust boundaries & authentication** — the one-diagram consolidation of ADRs 0006/0007/0008/0028: what crosses each boundary and with what credential, plus notes on defense-in-depth JWT validation, the only non-JWT endpoints, and fail-closed MCP.

**[test_architecture_er_sync.py](backend/tests/db/test_architecture_er_sync.py)** — the drift guard: every `Base.metadata` table must appear in the ER diagram and vice versa, with a self-sanity test so the guard can't pass vacuously. I confirmed the regex extracts exactly the 13 real tables. 3/3 pass; ruff/black/mypy clean; Bandit's B101 hits don't apply because the CI gate runs `-c pyproject.toml`, which excludes `backend/tests`.

**[CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md)** — Keep-a-Changelog format with the cadence policy in the header, an `[Unreleased]` section, and a curated `[1.0.0] — 2026-07-04` backfill covering the deployer/user-visible surface. **[runbook-faq.md](docs/runbook-faq.md)** got the matching release-checklist step.

One fix along the way: `mkdocs build --strict` rejects relative links pointing outside `docs/`, so all backend source links (including the one in the earlier ER section) now use absolute GitHub URLs, matching the runbook's existing convention. Strict build is clean.

Deferred per your earlier framing: the deeper threat-model doc stays a pre-marketplace item — the trust-boundary section is its seed.

Ready to branch (`docs/architecture-behavior-diagrams`), commit in logical units (`docs:` diagrams, `test:` drift guard, `docs:` changelog+policy), and open the PR with the milestone — just say go.

input.old_string:
```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> queued : run created (manual · schedule · trigger binding)
    queued --> running : worker picks up the task
    queued --> cancelled : user cancel — task revoked + cooperative start-check
    queued --> failed : dispatch / setup / target failure — or reaper past threshold (#309)
    running --> succeeded : execution completed (even when checks FAILED — run status is execution, not data quality)
    running --> failed : adapter raised — or worker died and the reaper swept it (#309)
    running --> cancelled : user cancel (DB status set; worker honours cooperatively)
    succeeded --> [*]
    failed --> [*]
    cancelled --> [*]
```
input.new_string:
```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> queued : created (manual · schedule · trigger)
    queued --> running : worker picks up
    queued --> cancelled : user cancel
    queued --> failed : dispatch/setup failure · reaper (#309)
    running --> succeeded : execution completed
    running --> cancelled : user cancel
    running --> failed : adapter raised · reaper (#309)
    succeeded --> [*]
    failed --> [*]
    cancelled --> [*]
```

- **`succeeded` means executed** — checks may still have failed; the data-quality outcome lives in `results.status`.
- **Cancel works on any non-terminal run:** the API sets `cancelled` and best-effort revokes the Celery task; the worker also honours the status cooperatively (start-check before executing).
- **The reaper (#309)** drives runs orphaned in `queued`/`running` past a threshold (task never published, or the worker died mid-run) to terminal `failed`.

content.text:
Good catch — it wasn't just cosmetic. Everything is fixed and verified now.

**What was odd:** the semicolon in the label `user cancel (DB status set; worker honours cooperatively)` terminated the Mermaid statement mid-line, so the leftovers rendered as four floating orphan boxes (`;`, `worker`, `honours`, `cooperatively)`) across the top of the state diagram. The other labels were also long enough to wrap into grey slabs bigger than the states themselves. The syntax validator happily passed all of it — the failure was only visible in the render.

**The fix:** the state diagram now has short edge labels (`user cancel`, `adapter raised · reaper (#309)` …) and the nuance moved into three prose bullets under it (succeeded ≠ checks passed, cancel semantics, reaper). I extracted the actual PNG render this time — it's compact and clean.

**Chasing that led to a second, worse bug you hadn't hit yet:** in *sequence* diagrams, Mermaid treats `#…;` as HTML-entity escape syntax, and every `#NNN` issue reference was silently eating its line — the run-lifecycle diagram rendered `never left stuck queued (` with `#227)` gone, and the orchestration diagram's entire polling-fallback note truncated to `polling fallback (`. GitHub uses the same engine, so both would have shipped mangled. Flowcharts and state diagrams are unaffected (`(#122)`/`(#309)` render fine there). Fix: the six refs inside the two sequence diagrams now use Mermaid's documented escape `#35;227` etc., which renders as `(#227)` — I verified each one comes through intact in the rendered SVG, along with the previously-swallowed gap-recovery text.

`mkdocs build --strict` is still green and no bare `#NNN` refs remain in any sequence block. The practical lesson baked into this session: for Mermaid, *valid* ≠ *renders correctly* — sequence-diagram text with `#` or `;` needs a render check, not just a syntax check. Want me to add that one-liner as a caution note next to the sync rule at the top of architecture.md?

input.new_string:
> Keep these diagrams in sync with the code. When a new component, datasource, integration, or DB table is added, update the relevant diagram in the same PR.
>
> ⚠️ **Mermaid gotcha — syntactically *valid* ≠ *renders correctly*.** In **sequence-diagram** text, `#` starts Mermaid's HTML-entity escape (`#35;` → `#`) and a stray `;` terminates a statement — a bare `#NNN` issue ref or an inline semicolon silently truncates the rendered line while the syntax check still passes. Write issue refs in sequence diagrams as `#35;NNN` (renders as `#NNN`; flowcharts/state/ER diagrams don't need this) and eyeball the rendered diagram before merging, not just the linter.
